birds caught across 83 sites over 30 years in Southern Cameroon, We compared
bird diversity in mature forest and cocoa using measures of alpha, beta and gamma
diversity, and we investigated the effect of cocoa farm shade and forest cover on
bird communities.

3, Gamma diversity was higher in cocoa than forest, though alpha diversity was simi-
lar indicating a higher dissimilarity (beta diversity) between cocaa farms. Cocoa
farms differed from forest in community composition, witha distinctive decrease
in relative abundance of insectivores, forest specialists and antfolowers and an
Increase in frugivores.

'4, Within cocoa farms, we found that farms with high shade cover in forested land-
scapes resulted in higher relative abundance and richness of sensitive forest spe-
ies; shady farms contained up to five times the proportion of forest specialists
than sunny farms.

5, Synthesis and applications. Sunny African cocoa farms were less able to support
sensitive bird gullds compared with shaded farms in forested landscapes. Our

findings support the notion that certain ecological and dietary guilds, such as

1 | INTRODUCTION

'Agricultural expansion is the main cause of terrestrial biodiversity
loss worldwide (Newbold et al, 2015). The tropes, partiulelysub-
Soharan Attica, hve the highest sk of biodiversity loss duet in
ed coverage of protected reas, low conservationspendingand igh
aaricultural growth (Kehoe et al, 2017). With agricultural demands
ojected to double inthe next decades (scharntke eta, 2012),
there san urgent need for strates that will combine agricultural
production and biodversity conservation. Tade-offsenst between,

ims need not be mutually exchisve:hiphyield fod production nd

Cocoa is the fastest expanding export-oriented crop inthe

'mass deforestation in countries such as ory Coast. where tis now

land (Maclea, 2017). In other countries such a Cameroon, the Sth

net usually under a thick forest canopy Rie & Greenberg, 2000),
Though shortterm yields may be higher in fullsun plantations.
shaded cocoa farms havea longer productive life pan and suffer

etal, 2011 However. the Government af Cameroon aims to triple
«c0c03 production by 2035 (Ordway eta, 2017), which may ead to
clearing of forested land for menocutures and conversion of shade
sown cocoa to sun monocultares (Andes et al, 2016; Sebeoth &
Harvey. 2007.

Cocoa apoforestry systems often maintain a high diversity of
rainforest shade trees that may resemble the rainforest theyre
placed (Bsseleun et al, 2013: Sonwa etal, 2007) Party due to
this, several studies have suggested that cocoa agroforestry sys

plantations (Bhagwat ea, 2008; Bisseleus eta, 2009; Techarntke
tal, 2011: Vergara & Badano, 2009). However, most stules on
ood are from the Neotropics and South-East Asian ther meta-

the capacity of Atfican cocoa agroecosystems to maintain blodver-
sity. Specialy, we know litle regarding vertebrate crmmurites,

{& Harvey, 2007: Sekerioga, 2012) The notable exceptions are
Wiltert eta. (2005) and Kupsch et al (2019). who surveyed birds
across a gradient af land-use intensiiation whieh inclded some
«cocoa plots and found that although species richness did not e-
crease with increasing habitat modification, community composition
was slnicanty affected, with a decrease in abundance of laae
bodied frugivores and terrestrial insectvores.

Factors affecting animal diversity in cocoe agroforestry sys
ems occur at two spatial scales: fam level (025-5 Ha) and and
'scape level. Within the fae, management actions such 28 shade
tree removal and pruning wil affect an anima community, Inthe
Neotropics, farms with dense, structurally diverse vegetation
have been shown to support a higher diversity of birds (Cassano
etal, 2009, ants (Piet etal, 2006) and amphibians (Dehewvels
fetal. 2014) At a landscape scale, animals are affected by habitat
connectivity as has been shown In Braz where farms in forested
areas support higher diversity of bids bats and frogs than farms
In disturbed non-forested landscapes (Cassano etal, 2009: Faria
tal, 2006)

'Our study isthe frst to specially examine Atlean cocoa fatms

itt for birds, We found that sensitive guilds such as for
est specialists and ant-olowers represented a larger propartion
'of the community in shady farms compared with full-sun farms,
and that these groupe occured at higher relative abundance io

Wie found thatthe caceaarms bird assemblage comprised
'more species than the forest assemblage. This could possibly be
'due tothe variability of habitat characteristics in cocoa farms.
which can range from ful-sun scrubby plantations to shaded
farms that are structurally sini to the forest Sonwa et al. 2007;
Ticharnke etal, 2011) Indeed the lower representativencss of
cocoa farms indicates that they were more dissimilar between each
other compared with forest sites, These findings support Sol
«ta (2015), who report increased beta diversity between second
ary forest sites than between undisturbed forest sites. The authors
argue thatthe higher betweensite beta diversity of disturbed for.
'ests may attenuate species loss ata larger scale. However, though
cocoa farms may contain moce species that are rate across the
metacommunity, these ar likely not forest- of rangesrestricted
species (Appendix $21 Indeed, there is an overall tend towaeds

conservation of certain bird guilds, we must aso prioritize protec-
on of undisturbed primary forests that provide habitat for spe-

The broad dliferences in community composition that we
found between cocoa farms and forest are consistent with it:
erature from across the topics. The shift fom forest to cocoa
results ina decrease in insectvores, forest specialists and ant
followers and an increase in frugvotes and nectarvores in the
'Neotropics (Faria eta. 2006: Rice & Greenberg. 2000) and Asia
(Maas eal, 2016; Marsden et al, 2006), Our findings conteibute
toa growing recogrition that species loss in forested systems Is.
linked to certain ecological guilds. Throughout tropical reals, is
tantly related species have evolutionary converged on similar be
haviour, suchas ant following and participating in mined-species

'We found thatthe community composition of bieds in cocoa
farms was signiicantly affected by shade and forest cover, with
an increased relative abundance of forest specialists, ant-followers
and mixed-lock species in shaded farms with high forest cover.
Forest speciaits are closely ted to vegetation structure (Powell
etal, 2085; Stratford & Stouffer 2013) especially withthe under
storey, whichis entirely removed in intensive cocoa plantations
(Kessler eta, 2005). Additional, habitat amount - propertion
of forest in landscape is important in determining bird abundance
and richness. and this effect may be more pronounced in understo=
rey or forest specialist species (Carrara et al. 2015: De Camargo
etal, 2018), explaining the inereased relative abundance and d-
versity ofthese bids with forest cover. Given the current rate of
land-use change forest beds are under severe teat and wl ly
undergo rapid species loss (Maas et al, 2009; Powell tal. 2015:
Sekercioglu, 2012)

tems. Adon, ants are affected by landscape-level processes,

(ur study provides strong evidence that Alcan cocoa planta-
tions canbe af value for conserving avian diversity, However, plan
tations need appropriate management if habitat Isto be provided
for forest bird communities. Low intensity shaded cocoa not only
provides habitat for forest beds and other vulnerable tara but can
also produce high velds and farmer income, comparable to more
lotensve systems, thanks to lower pest burdens longer produc-
tivelife span of trees and lower input costs (Armengot et al, 2016;
Clough eta, 2011; char ot a. 2013) This dua function of,
cocoa agroforestry systems allans with a and-shaving perspective,
In which agriculture Is managed 2 lw intensity and in favour of
biodiversity. However, our fests aio demonstrate that even the
shaist cocoa farms are not equivalent to forest. and therefore we
segue that within a land- sharing scenario there must he ates af fot-
lest preserved on the landscape. In concusion, to prevent extreme
elorestation and biodiversity loss in ane of the words diversity
hotspots. policymakers should atively encourage ecologically sus
talnable agricultural practices such as shaded cocoa agroforestry
that employs slence-based management
